coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive analytical strategy for malachite green determination in fish samples based on bright orange-emissive carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Qin
Cui, Yikun
Zhang, Longfei
Qian, Minjie
Xiao, Lixia
Yang, Ming
Yang, Zhen-Quan
Rao, Shengqi
Gong, Xiaojuan
Han, Jie
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel fluorescence method based on an orange-emissive carbon dots (O-CNDs) was for the first time reported for the rapid and ultrasensitive determination of malachite green (MG). The O-CNDs were simply produced via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of rhodamine B. The detection mechanism was based on the interaction between O-CNDs and MG, where the fluorescence of O-CNDs was suppressed by MG via a combined effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic suppressing. Under the optimum sensing conditions, the O-CNDs were found to display rapid reaction time (1.0 min), high selectivity and excellent sensitivity with two linear concentration ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–10.0 μmol L−1, and the corresponding detection limits were as low as 0.08 and 0.93 nmol L−1, respectively. The developed fluorescence method was successfully applied for the determination of MG in fish samples, including silver carp, blackfish, blunt-snout bream, common carp and grass carp, with satisfactory results; the recoveries were in a range of 96.0–104.8 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4.54 %. The results of this study validated the great potential of O-CNDs as an effective fluorescence sensing agent for MG monitoring in aquatic samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-CNDs
Fluorescence probe
Inner filter effect
Malachite green
Fish tissues

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: MG determination in food samples by O-CNDs-based fluorescence method
MG determination in food samples by HPLC analysis
Optimization of the parameters for MG sensing work
MG sensing performance of the O-CNDs
Comparison of the proposed analytical method with the previously reported methods
The quenching mechanism of O-CNDs towards MG

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A)The TEM image and (B) the particle size distribution histogram of the as-synthesised O-CNDs. (C) The MS spectrum of O-CNDs. (D) The FTIR spectrum of O-CNDs.

(A) The UV–vis absorption (green curve), excitation (red curve) and emission (blue line) spectra of O-CNDs. The inset is the photograph of O-CNDs aqueous solution obtained under UV irradiation. (B) The PL spectra of O-CNDs aqueous solution at various λex. The arrows indicating which y-axis represents the UV–vis and fluorescence data are now inserted in Fig. 2A (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) The effect of O-CNDs dosage on F0/F. The O-CNDs solutions were prepared in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 5.0) at a concentration range of 0.01‒1.0 mg mL−1. (B) The O-CNDs solutions were prepared in 10 mM, PBS solutions ranging from pH 3.0 to 11.0 and the O-CNDs concentrations were set at 0.1 mg mL−1. (C) The effect of reaction time on F0/F. The O-CNDs solution was prepared in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 5.0) at a concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1. For all the experiments, the amount of MG added was 10.0 μmol L−1.

(A) The PL spectra of a O-CNDs solution (2.0 mL, 0.1 mg mL−1) after the addition of various amounts of MG (1‒18: 0, 0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 10.0, 12.0, 14.0, 16.0, 20.0, 24.0, 28.0, 34.0, 42.0, 50.0, 66.0 μmol L−1). (B) and (C) are the SV plots of F0/F against the MG contents in the concentration range of 0.0‒0.1 and 0.1‒10.0 μmol L−1, respectively. The error bars indicate the standard deviations from five independent measurements (n = 5).

(A) The F0/F of O-CNDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) in the presence of MG and various possible interfering substances (0.1 mmol L−1). (B) The F0/F of the O-CNDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1)/MG (0.1 mmol L−1) mixture in the absence and in the presence of other potential interfering species (0.1 mmol L−1). The error bars are the standard deviations calculated from five independent experiments.

(A) The UV–vis absorption spectrum of MG (0.1 mmol L−1) and the excitation and emission spectra of O-CNDs (0.1 mg mL−1). (B) The fluorescence decay curve of a O-CNDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1) in the presence and in the absence of 10.0 μmol L−1 MG. The arrows indicating which y-axis represents the UV–vis and fluorescence data are now inserted in Fig. 6A. The spectra were normalized for ease of comparison.
Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure of O-CNDs and the principle for MG detection.
Comparison of the performance of different analytical methods for MG detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: BSA-AuNCs: bovine serum albumin-stabilised Au nanoclusters.
FQ-ITC: fluorescence quenching immunoaffinity test column.
icELISA: indirect competitive enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay.
OMCNs: ordered mesoporous carbon nanofiber arrays.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The results for MG detection in fish samples using the proposed O-CNDs-based analytic platform and HPLC.

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive analytical strategy for malachite green determination in fish samples based on bright orange-emissive carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, and School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu, 225001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University, Yangzhou, Jiangsu 225001, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Postdoctoral research fellow on visit to School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yangzhou University.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel fluorescence method based on an orange-emissive carbon dots (O-CNDs) was for the first time reported for the rapid and ultrasensitive determination of malachite green (MG). The O-CNDs were simply produced via a one-pot hydrothermal treatment of rhodamine B. The detection mechanism was based on the interaction between O-CNDs and MG, where the fluorescence of O-CNDs was suppressed by MG via a combined effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and dynamic suppressing. Under the optimum sensing conditions, the O-CNDs were found to display rapid reaction time (1.0 min), high selectivity and excellent sensitivity with two linear concentration ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–10.0 μmol L−1, and the corresponding detection limits were as low as 0.08 and 0.93 nmol L−1, respectively. The developed fluorescence method was successfully applied for the determination of MG in fish samples, including silver carp, blackfish, blunt-snout bream, common carp and grass carp, with satisfactory results; the recoveries were in a range of 96.0–104.8 % and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 4.54 %. The results of this study validated the great potential of O-CNDs as an effective fluorescence sensing agent for MG monitoring in aquatic samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) is a basic dye containing a triarylmethane group (Singh and Singh, 2020). It has been widely used for years as parasiticide and fungicide in fish farming industry due to its effective antimicrobial activity (Samiey and Toosi, 2010). However, literature evidence showed that the MG is of high toxicity, which can cause carcinogenic and genotoxic changes by introducing free radical generation, free DNA breaks and chromosomal aberrations (Srivastava et al., 2004; Mittelstaedt et al., 2004). The intake of MG into human body could also cause adverse effects on human liver, kidney, spleen and fertility rate (Singh and Singh, 2020). In light of these findings, the usage of MG in aquaculture has been banned in many countries, including US, Canada, European Union and China; however, the illegal use of MG is still prevalent due to its high effectiveness, low cost and commercial availability (Yang et al., 2020). Therefore, it is of high demand to develop a fast, simple and accurate analytical method for MG monitoring in aquatic products.

So far, various analytical methods have been developed to detect MG, including immunoassay (Chen et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2011), surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) (Fu et al., 2013), electrochemical detection (ED) (Yang et al., 2020), high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Gao et al., 2019; Hashemi et al., 2019; Ghasemi and Kaykhaii, 2016; Long et al., 2009), ultra-high performance liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (UHPLC–MS/MS) (Li et al., 2020) and fluorescence methods (Singh and Singh, 2020; Ju et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). Among them, the fluorescence methods, especially the fluorescence nanoprobes, stand out due to its rapidity, ease of operation and high sensitivity. For example, Ju et al. (Ju et al., 2018) reported a ratiometric fluorescence method for MG detection based on dual-emissive gold nanoclusters via dynamic interaction and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Luo et al. (Luo et al., 2019) reported fluorescence “off–on” detection of MG based on an RNA aptamer which could specially bind with MG. However, the researches in this area are still rare, and there is much room for further exploration and improvement.

Carbon dots (CNDs) are fascinating fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nmol L−1 (Baker and Baker, 2010). The CNDs were reported to exhibit good photo-stability, tuneable emission, excitation wavelengths (λex)-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behaviour (Zhai et al., 2012; Sun et al., 2013; Bourlinos et al., 2015). Due to theses superior features, the CNDs have attracted remarkable attention in various application fields, especially in sensing work (Ghereghlou et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2018, 2019; Shi et al., 2015) where the sensing mechanism is usually based on the fluorescence quenching of CNDs via either fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE). In comparison to the FRET mechanism, the IFE stands out with enhanced sensitivity and faster response since the changes in the fluorophore absorbance could transform exponentially into the fluorescence intensity change (Hu et al., 2019). So far, the IFE-based sensing system with CNDs as nanoprobes have been reported to be efficient in detecting various food additives, such as tartrazine (Ghereghlou et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021), sunset yellow (Yang et al., 2018), tannic acid (Yang et al., 2019), curcumin (Shi et al., 2015) and etc.

Very recently, few studies showed the possibility of using CNDs as nanoprobe for MG sensing via IFE. Singh et al. (Singh and Singh, 2020) reported a N doped CNDs for MG detection with a linear range of 10.0–80.0 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 5.16 μmol L−1 were achieved. Hu et al. (Hu et al., 2021) developed a Se, N and Cl co-doped CNDs for MG detection, achieving a linear range of 0.07–2.50 μmol L−1 and a detection limit of 21.0 nmol L−1. However, the usage of CNDs as nanoprobe for MG monitoring via IFE detection mechanism is still in the infant stage and the analytical performances of the existing CNDs-based nanoprobe still have much room to improve. Since both the optical properties and the spectral overlap between CNDs and MG can affect the quenching efficiency of an IFE-based sensing system, which in turn affects the detection sensitivity, and therefore, searching for suitable precursors to develop CNDs with desired spectral properties and enhanced analytical performance is highly necessary.

In this work, an orange-emissive CNDs (O-CNDs) was for the first time proposed for ultrasensitive determination of MG (Scheme 1 ). The O-CNDs was prepared via a one-pot hydrothermal method with rhodamine B as the only precursor. The as-synthesized O-CNDs not only have good overlap with the excitation and emission light of O-CNDs, leading to the fluorescence quenching of O-CNDs via IFE, but also have a high quantum yield of 46.2 %. In light of this observation, a fluorescence probe based on O-CNDs was developed for MG determination. The sensing parameters, including pH, reaction time and O-CNDs dosage, were optimised. The analytical method was fully validated by studying its linearity, detection limit, selectivity, anti-interference ability, standard uncertainty and trueness. Finally, the proposed analytical method was successfully applied for MG determination in fish samples. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study on using O-CNDs-based nanoprobe for MG determination, which would undoubtedly add credibility to the use of CNDs as sensing agent in food quality monitoring.

All chemicals and reagents were of analytical grade or above and no further purification was needed before use. The rhodamine B dye, fructose, sucrose, glucose and all amino acids were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The other chemicals used in this work were all obtained from Beijing Chemical Aladdin chemicals (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was prepared by using a Millipore-Q system (Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout the experiments. The PBS solution (10 mM, pH 5.0) were adjusted from pH 3.0–11.0 with Na3PO4, Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4.

The O-CNDs was synthesised according to a previously reported method with slight modification (Tang et al., 2019). In detail, 0.50 g of rhodamine B dye was mixed with 20.00 mL of ultrapure water and finely dispersed with the assistance of ultra-sonication. Then, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 160 °C for 2 h. After naturally cooling down, the reaction resultant was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected and dialysed against 1.0 L ultrapure water through a 500–1000 Da MWCO dialysis membrane. The dialysis process was conducted over a course of 3 days with constant magnetic stirring applied, and fresh ultrapure water was recharged into the beaker every 24 h. Finally, the purified solution containing O-CNDs was lyophilised to get the dry O-CNDs product.

A CM100 electron microscope (Philips, Eindhoven, Netherlands) was used to perform the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement. An Elementar Analysen systeme vario EL cube organic elemental analyser (Hanau, Germany) was used for the elemental analysis. A Varian 670-IR + 610-IR FTIR spectrometer (Lincolnshire, IL, USA) was used for the Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) measurement. A UV-2550 absorption spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) and a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) were used to acquire the UV–vis and PL spectra, respectively. A FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Co., Ltd., England) was used to carry out the fluorescence decay measurements. The quantum yield measurement was carried out according to a previously reported method with quinine sulphate as a reference (Bourlinos et al., 2015). The detail experimental information is deposited in Supplementary Material.

Five kinds of fish samples, including silver carp, blackfish, blunt-snout bream, common carp and grass carp, were obtained from the local food markets at Yangzhou City, China and raised in water to keep live before be treated. The fish samples were treated according to a previously reported literature with slight modification (Hu et al., 2021). Briefly, 5.0 g of the fish back muscle was finely minced and transferred into a 100 mL beaker. Then, 30.0 mL of acetonitrile was added to mix and subjected to ultra-sonication for 1 h, and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was collected, filtered through cellulose membrane (0.45 μm, 13 mm id) and stored at 4 °C for further analyses.

To study the linearity of the proposed method, various amounts of MG were step-wisely added into a O-CNDs solution (0.1 mg mL−1, 2.0 mL), making the added amounts of MG in a concentration of 0.0–66 μmol L−1. Unless otherwise stated, the O-CNDs solutions were prepared in PBS solution (10 mM, pH 5.0).

The fluorescence intensities of O-CNDs in the absence and in the presence of MG were measured at 575 nm (λex = 555 nm). The three times the standard deviation rule (3σ/K SV ) was used to determine the detection limit, where twelve independent measurements of the blank O-CNDs solution were used to calculate σ; the limit of quantity was calculated according to 10σ/K SV (Yu et al., 2013). The standard deviations were obtained from five independent measurements.

The selectivity of the proposed method was studied by observing the fluorescence response of the O-CNDs solution towards the various species that could potentially exist in the fish samples. In detail, the MG, four kinds of anions (Cl−, NO3 −, NO2 −, and HCO3 −), six kinds of cations (K+, Na+, NH4 +, Mg2+, Fe3+ and Ca2+), eight kinds of amino acids (serine, glycine, L-methionine, alanine, histidine, DL-cysteine, glutamate and threonine), five kinds of dyes (curcumin, amaranth, indigo, brilliant blue and quinolone yellow) and the other small molecules (fructose, sucrose, glucose) and were mixed with 2.0 mL O-CNDs (0.1 mg mL−1), respectively. The added contents of all the potential interferences were 0.1 mmol L−1. The fluorescence intensities of O-CNDs before and after the addition of the interferences were measured at 575 nm (λex = 555 nm).

The anti-interference ability of the proposed method was studied by observing the fluorescence response of the O-CNDs/MG mixture solution towards the above mentioned potential interferences. In detail, the MG was mixed with a 2.0 mL O-CNDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) at a concentration of 0.1 mmol L−1. Then, the potential interferences were added into the O-CNDs/MG mixture with the concentration of each interfering species setting at 0.1 mmol L−1. The fluorescence intensities of O-CNDs/MG before and after the addition of the above possible interferences were measured at 575 nm (λex = 555 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG determination in food samples by O-CNDs-based fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: For the MG quantification in the above fish samples, 20.0 μL of the extraction of each fish sample was mixed with 2.0 mL of O-CNDs (0.1 mg mL−1), respectively. The fluorescence intensities of O-CNDs were recorded at 575 nm (λex = 555 nm) before and after mixing with the sample extraction.

The quantitative recoveries were investigated to check the accuracy of the proposed method. In detail, known amounts of MG were spiked into the extraction of each food sample at concentrations of 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 μmol L−1, respectively. Then, the contents of MG in spiked sample extractions were determined by using the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG determination in food samples by HPLC analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The contents of MG in fish samples were also determined by HPLC according to a previously published method with slight modification (Hu et al., 2021). An Agilent HPLC 1200 series (Santa Clara, CA, USA) with a photodiode array detector was used for the measurement. The chromatographic separation was carried out by using a C18 column (150 × 4.6 mm id stainless steel) from Waters Corporation (Milford, MA, USA). The UV detection was monitored at 618 nm. An isocratic elution program was used for all separations with a mobile phase consisting of 90:10 (v/v) acetonitrile and 10.0 mM ammonium acetate. The flow rate was set as 1.0 mL min−1, the temperature was set at 25 °C, and the injection volume was 20.0 μL. For each specific sample, the HPLC analysis was run for triplicates, the average value was recorded as the MG content in sample. The procedure for sample preparation was the same as that used for the above O-CNDs-based fluorescence method.

The morphology and size distribution information was studied by TEM and the corresponding TEM image was displayed in Fig. 1 . It was seen that the black dots, which represent the as-synthesised O-CNDs species, were well dispersed on the TEM image (Fig. 1A) without apparent aggregation. Their diameters were distributed in a range of 3.2 − 5.6 nm with the average population size at 4.3 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1B). In addition, the MS spectrum of O-CNDs was acquired (Fig. 1C), and it was found that the largest and the highest mass of the as-synthesised O-CNDs species were 1553 and 442 m/z, respectively.

The elemental composition of the O-CNDs was studied by elemental analysis. As shown in Table S1, the O-CNDs were composed of C 47.21 %, H 6.57 %, O 32.62 % and N 13.6 %. The surface functionalities of O-CNDs were identified by FTIR. As shown in Fig. 1D, a broad absorption band at around 3445 cm−1 was assigned to −OH stretching vibration. The stretching vibrations at 2358 cm−1 and 1077 cm−1 were attributed to CN group. The absorption at 2975 cm−1 and 2926 cm−1 were corresponding to the stretching vibration of CH. The absorption bands at 1590 cm−1 was originated from the stretching vibration of CO/C = C bond. In addition, several weak absorption peaks ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CC (1412 cm-1, 1338 cm−1) and CO (1180 cm−1) of the aromatic ring were observed. The FTIR spectrum of the raw materials was also acquired for comparison in order to evaluate the evolution of the surface functionalities of O-CNDs. In essence, the N was successfully doped into O-CNDs and the as-synthesised O-CNDs were functionalised with carbonyl, amide and hydroxyl groups, and therefore the O-CNDs was of good hydrophilicity.

To study the optical properties of O-CNDs, the UV–vis absorption and PL spectra of the O-CNDs were acquired. As seen from the UV–vis absorption spectrum of O-CNDs (green curve, Fig. 2 A), the O-CNDs had two typical absorption peaks at 350 nm and 550 nm with the former one ascribed to n–π* transition of CO bond (Liu et al., 2017), and the later one attributed to n–π* electron transitions involving lone pairs of the edge nitrogen atoms of the heptazine units (Zhang et al., 2017). The excitation spectrum of O-CNDs (red curve, Fig. 2A) showed that the O-CNDs had an optimal excitation peak at 555 nm. When excited at 555 nm, the O-CNDs showed an emission peak at 575 nm (blue curve, Fig. 2A). It was observed that the O-CNDs solution emitted intense orange colour under UV illumination. The good overlap between the excitation spectrum and the absorption spectrum of O-CNDs indicated that the surface structures and conditions well support the orange emissive feature of O-CNDs. In addition, the O-CNDs showed excitation-independent behaviour with no obvious shift of the emission peak position as the excitation wavelength changed from 520 to 580 nm. The quantum yield of O-CNDs measured by a comparative method (Fig. S1) was calculated to be 46.2 % by using quinine sulphate as the standard.

The use of nanotechnology could threaten biosafety once the deposited nanomaterials entered the ecological chain, and thus one has to pay much attention to the cytotoxicity of the nanomaterials before usage for application. The cytotoxicity of O-CNDs was studied by MTT with HepG-2 cells (human hepatoma carcinoma cell) (Fig. S2). The detail experimental conditions for cytotoxicity studies are deposited in supplementary material. It was observed that the viability of HepG-2 cells were above 95 % after being incubated for 48 h with various concentrations of O-CNDs. This indicates that the as-synthesised O-CNDs were of low cytotoxicity and therefore, the usage of O-CNDs for MG assay was of biological safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the parameters for MG sensing work
ce:section.ce:para: Since a small amount of O-CNDs would give a narrow linear range, but an excess usage of O-CNDs would result in a poor sensitivity, the influence of O-CNDs concentration (0.01–1.0 mg mL−1) on the detection work was studied. As displayed in Fig. 3 A and Fig. S3, both the F0 and F0/F fluctuate O-CNDs concentration with the optimum dosage of O-CNDs for the MG sensing work was 0.1 mg mL−1.

The F0/F at various pH (3.0–11.0) were evaluated to optimise the pH. As displayed in Fig. 3B, the F0/F fluctuated with the pH ranged from 3.0–11.0, and the highest F0/F was obtained when pH was at 5.0. To further investigate the effect of pH on the interaction between O-CNDs and MG, the zeta potentials of O-CNDs before and after mixing with MG were measured at different pHs. As indicated by Fig. S4A, both the zeta potential of O-CNDs and O-CNDs/MG fluctuate with pH. Fig. S4B showed the relative changes of the zeta potential of O-CNDs caused by the addition of MG are calculated. The highest change of the zeta potential of O-CNDs after mixing with MG was observed at pH 5.0. Therefore, the optimal pH for MG sensing in the whole detection process was chosen as 5.0.

The optimal response time was also evaluated to clarify fluorescence variations as a function of reaction time. As shown in Fig. 3C, the F0/F increased sharply after the MG and O-CNDs was mixed for 1.0 min and kept stable afterwards. Based on the above observance, 1.0 min as the optimal reaction time was used for the subsequent measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MG sensing performance of the O-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: With the superior optical properties, the O-CNDs were used as nanoprobe to detect MG via fluorescence quenching. Different contents of MG were step-wisely introduced into the O-CNDs solution, and the corresponding fluorescence intensities of O-CNDs/MG mixture were measured. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of O-CNDs gradually declined as the concentration of MG increased. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F, where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of O-CNDs and O-CNDs/MG, respectively) was found to be linear with the content of MG in two concentration ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–10.0 μmol L−1 (Fig. 4B). The concentration dependence of F0/F was fitted by the following Stern − Volmer equation (F0/F = 1+KSV [MG], where [MG] and KSV are the concentration of MG and the Stern − Volmer constant, respectively). The KSV were calculated to be 1.142 × 106 and 0.10 × 106 L mol−1 with a determination coefficient R2 of 0.9998 and 0.9997, respectively. The detection limits estimated according to the 3σ (signal-to-noise ratio) rule, i.e., (3σ/K SV ), were calculated to be 0.08 and 0.93 nmol L−1, respectively. The limits of quantity (10σ/K SV ) were calculated to be 0.26 and 3.07 nmol L−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the proposed analytical method with the previously reported methods
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of this method was compared to the existed method and was shown in Table 1 . It was found that the detection limit of this proposed method was much lower than the previously reported HPLC-UV (Faraji et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2019; Hashemi et al., 2019), ED (Yang et al., 2020), SERS (Fu et al., 2013), immunoassay (Chen et al., 2018; Dong et al., 2021; Shen et al., 2011) and spectrofluorimetry methods (Singh and Singh, 2020; Ju et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). More importantly, the detection limit of this proposed method was even comparable to UHPLC–MS/MS method (Li et al., 2020) with additional merits of low cost, fast detection and ease of operation. The results clearly demonstrate that the proposed analytical strategy could be potentially used for the sensitive detection of MG.

To determine the feasibility of the O-CNDs as fluorescence nanoprobe for real sample analysis, it is of high significance to explore its selectivity and anti-interference ability. Fig. 5 A displayed the fluorescence responses of O-CNDs towards various possible interferences, including anions, cations, amino acids, organic dyes and the other small molecules. It was shown that only MG significantly suppressed the fluorescence intensity of O-CNDs while the other possible interferences showed unobvious suppressing effect on O-CNDs fluorescence, indicating the high selectivity of O-CNDs towards MG sensing. The anti-interference ability of O-CNDs was investigated by adding each other possible interference into the mixture solution of O-CNDs and MG, where the concentrations of MG and the other possible interference were 0.1 mM. As shown by Fig. 5B, the fluorescence response revealed negligible interference to the selectivity of O-CNDs, indicating that various possible interferences could coexist with MG without obvious interfering in an analytical system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The quenching mechanism of O-CNDs towards MG
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism of O-CNDs was carefully studied. As illustrated in Fig. 6 A, good overlapping between the absorption spectrum of MG and the excitation and emission spectra of O-CNDs was observed, which indicates that both the excitation and emission light of O-CNDs can be effectively absorbed by MG. As such, the fluorescence quenching was resulted from IFE. To further clarify the interaction between O-CNDs and MG, the fluorescence decay profiles of O-CNDs and O-CNDs/MG mixture were studied (Fig. 6B). As shown in Table S2, the average fluorescence lifetime of O-CNDs decreased from 6.12 to 4.86 ns after the addition of MG, indicating the dynamic interaction between O-CNDs and MG. In essence, the quenching mechanism of O-CNDs by MG was a combined effect of IFE and dynamic suppression.

In light of the high selectivity and anti-interference ability of O-CNDs, the proposed analytic platform was used to detect MG in five kinds of fish samples (silver carp, blackfish, blunt-snout bream, common carp and grass carp) using standard addition method. As shown in Table 2 , no MG was detected in the fish samples. The spiked recoveries ranged from 96.0%–104.8%, and the RSDs (standard uncertainty) were less than 4.54 %, which indicates that our proposed analytical strategy was of high accuracy. To further demonstrate the method accuracy (trueness assessment) of the proposed analytical method, the HPLC method was carried out for comparison. The chromatographs of the MG standard and the extracted samples were displayed in Fig. S5. The detection and quantification limits were calculated according to 3 and 10 times the σ rule were 0.41 and 1.34 μmol L−1, respectively. The results also showed the absence of MG in the fish samples, which unambiguously verified the feasibility and dependability of using O-CNDs as a nanoprobe for MG determination in complex aquatic samples.

A simple, highly selective and ultrasensitive fluorescence method was for the first time developed for the rapid determination of MG by using orange-emissive O-CNDs as nanoprobe. The sensing mechanism was based on IFE and dynamic interaction between MG and O-CNDs. The linearity for MG detection was found in two concentration ranges of 0.01–0.1 and 0.1–10.0 μmol L −1, respectively, and the detection limits were measured to be as low as 0.08 and 0.93 nmol L−1, respectively. The proposed fluorescence method was able to quantitatively determinate MG in fish samples with satisfactory results. This work illustrates the potential utility of O-CNDs for MG monitoring in aquatic products.

Qin Hu, Zhen-quan Yang, and Jie: Han designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Xiaojuan Gong: helped design the characterization work. Qin Hu, Yikun Cui, and Lixia Xiao: performed the experiments. Qin Hu and Yikun Cui: analysed data.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial supports from Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province, China (BK20200949), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671625), National Natural Science Foundation of China (21922202), Natural Science Fund for Colleges and Universities in Jiangsu Province, China (19KJB150042, 20KJA550002), Opening Foundation of Jiangsu Dairy Bioengineering Technology Research Centre (KYRY2019002), Yangzhou Key R&D Project (Social Development), China (2020) (YZ2020063) and LvYang Golden Phoenix Plan, Yangzhou City (2019) are gratefully acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jfca.2021.104032.
